prompt:
Release engineering ticket, severity raised after Thursday. mkrelease.sh stages release bundles out of finished build trees (VERSION file, MANIFEST of `<path><TAB><max-kb>` lines, artifact paths never contain whitespace) into `<outdir>/rel-<version>/` with a RECEIPT. It runs under `set -e`, which the whole team has been treating as a guarantee that a half-done release cannot happen. This month it happened three different ways:

1. A tree rsync'd from CI was missing assets/logo.png. The log cheerfully says `mkrelease: staged assets/logo.png` — for a file that does not exist — then the run face-plants with bash unbound-variable noise somewhere in the receipt step and exits 1. Nothing on stderr names the actual problem, and a half-populated rel-7.3.1/ was left sitting in the outdir for the deploy job to find.

2. media/big.bin came out of the build at 2048 bytes against its 1-KB manifest cap. The `mkrelease: over budget` warning is right there in stderr — immediately followed by `sealed` and `release OK`, exit 0. The pipeline shipped it.

3. Someone pointed the tool at a half-synced tree with no VERSION. It logged `mkrelease: version ` (blank), created a directory literally named `rel-`, and reported release OK. A tree with no MANIFEST at all is even better: instant success, empty receipt.

I want failures to actually fail: the right message on stderr, the right nonzero exit, and no rel-* directory left behind in the outdir on ANY failure — partial bundles are exactly how we got burned. The happy path must not change: its stdout log lines are grepped by the release dashboards and must stay byte-for-byte identical. Exit codes and messages for each failure are pinned in test_mkrelease.sh — that file is the contract, do not modify it; fix mkrelease.sh itself. Boxes involved include the macOS build minis, so keep whatever you touch portable.
